extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
79-year-old African American retired postal worker. Widowed in 2024 when his wife Dorothy passed from pancreatic cancer. Previously lived independently in Jacksonville, FL. Now lives with his adult daughter Margaret Washington-Clarke (age 52) in Decatur, GA (metro Atlanta), who holds his healthcare power of attorney.

## Clinical Scenario
Harold was diagnosed with early-stage Alzheimer's disease in 2022 by his neurologist in Jacksonville. He was managing reasonably well with his wife's support — on donepezil, with PCP visits for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes (well controlled). After Dorothy's death in mid-2024, Harold's cognitive decline accelerated. He had a fall at home, forgot medications, and got lost driving to the grocery store. Margaret moved him to Georgia in late 2024.

In Georgia, Harold established with a new PCP and a geriatric neurologist at Emory. His MMSE dropped from 22/30 (mild) to 17/30 (moderate) over 6 months. He was switched from donepezil to a donepezil-memantine combination. His diabetes and hypertension management continued with his new PCP. He had one ED visit for a mechanical fall (no fracture, CT head negative).

Margaret, as his authorized representative, uses a patient-facing app to access Harold's records across both the Florida and Georgia systems.

## Sites
- **Riverside Internal Medicine** — PCP in Jacksonville, FL (2015–2024). Managed hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, annual wellness.
- **First Coast Neurology Associates** — Neurologist in Jacksonville, FL (2022–2024). Alzheimer's diagnosis, initial donepezil management, cognitive assessments.
- **DeKalb Primary Care** — New PCP in Decatur, GA (2024–present). Continued chronic disease management post-move.
- **Emory Cognitive Neurology Center** — Geriatric neurologist in Atlanta, GA (2025–present). Alzheimer's progression monitoring, medication changes.

Four sites across two states (FL, GA). Tests Permission Ticket constraints on **jurisdictions** (FL vs GA), **organizations** (4 providers), **periods** (pre-move Florida records vs post-move Georgia records), and **scopes** (can filter to just cognitive assessments or just diabetes management).

note.text: One-year follow-up (enc-008): Disease confirmed stable on donepezil 10 mg therapy. MMSE 23/30, one-point change from 24/30 over approximately one year, consistent with expected stabilization on cholinesterase inhibitor. Patient remains in the mild stage with preserved basic ADLs and partially preserved IADLs.

Final visit (enc-011): Disease progressed from mild to moderate stage. MMSE declined from 23/30 to 19/30 over approximately 18 months. Accelerated decline temporally associated with loss of wife Dorothy (June 2024), disruption of established daily routines, and loss of medication/supervision support. Patient now requires prompting for medications, sometimes forgets to eat, has occasional nighttime confusion. No longer driving. Donepezil 10 mg continued; addition of memantine recommended to receiving neurologist.

resource.conclusion: Mild generalized cortical atrophy. Bilateral hippocampal volume loss, left greater than right. No acute infarcts. No mass lesions. No subdural collections. Small-vessel ischemic changes, Fazekas grade 1.

note.text: MMSE 23/30 administered via telemedicine. Recall: 1/3, orientation to date: missed day and date (-2), serial sevens: -1. Registration, naming, repetition, 3-stage command, reading, writing, visuospatial: intact.

referenceRange.text: 0-30; higher scores indicate better cognitive function

interpretation.text: Decline of 4 points from 23/30 approximately 18 months prior; consistent with progression from mild to mild-moderate stage Alzheimer's disease

note.text: MMSE 19/30. Recall: 0/3. Orientation: lost 4 points (missed day, date, month, and floor). Attention/serial sevens: 2/5. Three-step command: lost 1 point. Prior MMSE 23/30 approximately 18 months ago (2023-04-11). Accelerated decline temporally associated with loss of spouse and disruption of daily routines.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 5 mg by mouth once daily at bedtime. Titrate to 10 mg daily after 4 weeks if tolerated.
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 5 mg by mouth once daily at bedtime.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 10 mg by mouth once daily at bedtime, starting tonight (2022-05-17).
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 1 tablet (10 mg) by mouth once daily at bedtime.

reasonCode.text: Initial neurology consultation for progressive memory impairment; Alzheimer's disease diagnosed

extension.valueMarkdown: Harold Washington, age 75, was referred by Dr. Cynthia Okafor for evaluation of progressive memory impairment noted over the preceding several months.
Dr. Patel's office called Harold and Dorothy to assess tolerance of donepezil 5 mg daily, started four weeks ago.

reasonCode.text: One-year cognitive monitoring follow-up for Alzheimer's disease

extension.valueMarkdown: Harold, age 76, connected via telemedicine video visit with Dr. Patel for his approximately one-year follow-up since Alzheimer's diagnosis.

reasonCode.text: Final visit prior to relocation; transition of care referral to Georgia neurology

extension.valueMarkdown: Harold, age 78, presented for his final visit at First Coast Neurology before relocating to Decatur, Georgia. Margaret Washington-Clarke accompanied him.

code.text: Referral to geriatric neurology in Atlanta/Decatur area for transition of care

note.text: Referral letter prepared for geriatric neurologist in Atlanta/Decatur area. Recommendations: (1) Re-evaluation with full neurocognitive battery within 3 months, (2) Consider adding memantine or switching to combination donepezil 10 mg/memantine ER therapy, (3) Continue MMSE monitoring every 3-4 months, (4) Advance care planning discussion given progressive disease trajectory. Comprehensive care summary provided including all prior MRI reports, cognitive testing history, and medication records.
